name: Jimmy Garrison
abstract: James Emory Garrison (March 3, 1934 – April 7, 1976) was an American jazz double bassist. He is best remembered for his association with John Coltrane from 1961 to 1967.

concerts.label: New Year's Eve Jazz at Carnegie Hall featuring John Coltrane, Thelonious Monk, Sonny Rollins, Nina Simone and George Crater
McCoy Tyner Trio with the New Art Ensemble and the Pharoah Sanders Sextet
Laura Nyro, The Rascals,  Alice Coltrane, and Pharoah Sanders

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: At the time we played the Plugged Nickel… I remember Jimmy Garrison was playing and he'd found a way to play... The music that John was playing then…
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: So Trane finally brought Jimmy Garrison into the band… I said, 'Get Jimmy Garrison' ... I called up Jimmy Garrison... so we got a trio together
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: We’re playing at a place called La Tête de l’Art... when we did that stuff... playing with the trio, McCoy and Garrison.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Carter
about_quotes.evidence: we got together... with Jimmy Garrison on base... just with the 8 piece group, we made that 'Further Definitions.'

about_quotes.speaker: Tyner McCoy
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] So when you join John’s band, you already know Jimmy Garrison. [INTERVIEWEE] Oh yeah, I knew Jimmy.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tyner McCoy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Konitz Lee
about_quotes.evidence: the record I made at the Half Note with Warne, Paul Motian, Jimmy Garrison, and Bill Evans
about_quotes.doc_title: Konitz Lee — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
